name: Ron Carter
description: American jazz bassist, cellist, and composer (born 1937)
abstract: Ronald Levin Carter (born May 4, 1937) is an American jazz double bassist. His appearances on 2,221 recording sessions make him the most-recorded jazz bassist in history.

quotes.text: Just two interesting kids from New Orleans. I had Raford (?) come by one day and play him some , uh … he was playing baritone or something like that with Hamp's band. He was torn between playing baritone and tenor, you know. I said, "Raf, come on to my house." So he came by and I played him some Don Byas and Slam Stewart, a 1944 concert at Carnegie Hall, and man, the eyebrows went up here. "I'm not asking you to play like that; I'm just showing you what kind of choices you have. And if you don't know Don Byas as a choice, just listen to him as a choice, you know." I had Wynton come by and he asked me about playing melodies. He wanted to know how Miles does that. So I said, "Well, Miles can do that because he understands what notes in the melody don't need to be there as to maintain the integrity of the melody."
quotes.caption: Recalling a 1944 Don Byas concert at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Ron Carter — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Herbie Hancock
about_quotes.evidence: Ron Carter the bassist was there... And so we kicked off one of the tunes... we played there, and then we recorded the album “Seven Steps to Heaven,” and the following week we played our first gig.
about_quotes.doc_title: Herbie Hancock interviewed by Michael Woods, New York City, New York, November 18, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Dick Katz
about_quotes.evidence: I hired Thad Jones and Jim Hall and Ron Carter and Pete LaRoca and myself. And we went in and did this.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dick Katz interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 7, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: Jones Hank
about_quotes.evidence: That consisted of Ron Carter on bass, Tony Williams on drums, and myself.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jones Hank — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: George Benson
about_quotes.evidence: You have – you’re doing Giblet Gravy. You have Herbie Hancock on piano, Ron Carter, bass... I didn’t realize how important he was in my career. He dressed up all my music... He had rhythms that led me from one thing to another and dressed it up.
about_quotes.doc_title: George Benson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Tyner McCoy
about_quotes.evidence: So you got that rhythm section and then on the other side you’ve got Eddie Gomez and Jack DeJohnette... I’m physically playing with the guy so I never hire a guy to do a recording with me unless I get a chance to play with him
about_quotes.doc_title: Tyner McCoy — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jim Hall
about_quotes.evidence: we were working at The Half Note with, with Art Farmer’s group... It was Ron and... I think, there’s one of those clubs I played with Ron Carter... We played at this club called The Guitar. I’d forgotten about that. As a duo.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jim Hall — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
